skill.name: 2d-games
skill.description: 2D game development principles. Sprites, tilemaps, physics, camera.
skill.summary: A comprehensive guide for building 2D games covering sprite systems, tilemap design, physics implementation, and camera techniques.
skill.author: sickn33

All findings are FALSE POSITIVES - the flagged content consists of documentation text (description strings and markdown headers) with no actual cryptographic code. This skill contains only educational documentation about 2D game development principles with no executable code, network access, or security-sensitive operations.

content.user_title: Build 2D Games with Sprite and Physics Systems
content.value_statement: Creating 2D games requires understanding multiple interconnected systems. This skill provides structured guidance for sprites, tilemaps, physics, and camera implementation.
actual_capabilities: Guide sprite atlas organization and animation frame sequencing
Design tilemap layers with auto-tiling for terrain systems
Implement 2D collision detection using appropriate shapes
Create smooth camera follow systems with look-ahead behavior
Apply genre-specific patterns for platformers and top-down games
Identify and avoid common 2D development anti-patterns
limitations: Does not provide engine-specific implementation code
Does not cover 3D game development techniques
Does not include audio or music implementation guidance
Does not address multiplayer or networking systems

use_cases.title: Indie Game Prototyping
use_cases.description: Rapidly prototype 2D game mechanics using established patterns for sprites, physics, and camera systems.

use_cases.title: Game Design Education
use_cases.description: Learn foundational 2D game development principles including animation timing, collision shapes, and screen shake techniques.

use_cases.title: Technical Art Direction
use_cases.description: Establish visual clarity guidelines for sprite atlases, layering, and pixel communication in 2D games.

prompt_templates.title: Sprite Animation Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me set up a sprite animation system for a 2D character. I need guidance on atlas organization, frame rate selection (8-24 FPS), and pivot point placement for rotation.

prompt_templates.title: Tilemap Layer Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a tilemap system with multiple layers for a 2D game. Include background, terrain, props, and foreground layers with appropriate collision handling.

prompt_templates.title: 2D Physics Collision
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend collision shapes for my 2D game objects. I have rectangular platforms, circular collectibles, and character sprites that need capsule colliders.

prompt_templates.title: Camera Follow Implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement a camera follow system with look-ahead behavior for a 2D platformer. Include screen shake effects for impact moments with 50-200ms duration.

output_examples.input: How should I organize sprite animations for a character with idle, walk, and jump states?
output: Create a sprite atlas grouping all character animations together
Set frame rates: idle (8 FPS), walk (12 FPS), jump (16 FPS)
Use consistent pivot points at character center for all animations
Apply squash and stretch on jump landing for impact emphasis

output_examples.input: What collision shape works best for a 2D platformer character?
output: Use capsule colliders for smooth movement on slopes
Simplify complex sprite shapes to basic geometric forms
Separate physics layers for player, enemies, and environment
Implement fixed timestep for consistent collision detection

best_practices: Use texture atlases to combine sprites and reduce draw calls
Simplify collision shapes for better physics performance
Apply smooth camera following with diminishing shake intensity
anti_patterns: Storing textures separately instead of using atlases
Using complex polygon collision when simple shapes suffice
Implementing pixel-perfect physics that causes jittery movement

faq.question: What sprite size should I use for my 2D game?
faq.answer: Common sizes are 16x16 for retro pixel art, 32x32 for modern indie games, and 64x64 for detailed characters. Choose based on your art style and target resolution.

faq.question: How do I prevent camera jitter in my 2D platformer?
faq.answer: Use smooth camera following with interpolation, avoid pixel-perfect physics coupling, and implement dead zones to prevent micro-adjustments during small movements.

faq.question: What frame rate works best for 2D sprite animations?
faq.answer: 8-12 FPS for subtle idle animations, 12-16 FPS for walk cycles, and 16-24 FPS for fast action sequences. Lower frame rates create a stylized retro feel.

faq.question: Should I use box or capsule colliders for my character?
faq.answer: Capsule colliders are better for platformers as they handle slopes smoothly. Box colliders work well for top-down games with grid-based movement.

faq.question: How can I reduce draw calls in my 2D game?
faq.answer: Combine sprites into texture atlases, batch sprites using the same material, and use automatic sprite packing tools available in your game engine.

faq.question: What is coyote time and should I implement it?
faq.answer: Coyote time allows jumping briefly after walking off a platform edge. It improves player experience in platformers and is recommended for most games.
